name: Raspberry Pi 5
tagline: The most powerful Raspberry Pi yet — a full Linux computer on a single board with 4K output
description: The Raspberry Pi 5 is a full-featured single-board computer running a 64-bit Linux OS (Raspberry Pi OS). Unlike Arduino, it is not a microcontroller — it runs a complete operating system and can run Python programs, host web servers, process camera feeds with AI, and interface with hardware via its 40-pin GPIO header. It features a quad-core Arm Cortex-A76 CPU, PCIe connectivity, and dual 4K display output.

specs.additionalFeatures: PCIe 2.0 x1, UART, I2C, SPI, Camera/Display CSI/DSI

pinout.name: GPIO Pins
pinout.desc: General-purpose 3.3V I/O — can be digital in/out, PWM, I2C, SPI, UART

wiringNote: All GPIO pins are 3.3V logic — 5V will damage them. Use a 5A USB-C power supply; anything less causes throttling. GPIO does NOT have built-in analog input — use an ADC (e.g., MCP3008) for analog sensors.
libraryInstall: Pre-installed on Raspberry Pi OS. Run: sudo apt update && sudo apt install python3-gpiozero
codeSnippet:
# Python — Blink LED on GPIO17
from gpiozero import LED
from time import sleep
led = LED(17)
while True:
    led.on()
    sleep(1)
    led.off()
    sleep(1)

# Read a button on GPIO27:
# from gpiozero import Button
# btn = Button(27)
# btn.wait_for_press()
# print("Button pressed!")
commonMistakes: Using a power supply below 5A → Pi 5 throttles CPU and warns about insufficient power
Connecting 5V signals to GPIO → instantly damages the GPIO bank (3.3V only)
Not using a heatsink/fan → Pi 5 throttles at high loads without active cooling
Pulling SD card without proper shutdown → corrupts the filesystem
Expecting real-time hardware control like Arduino — Pi OS is not real-time, use a co-processor or Pico
